text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: Genitourinary Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up
REASON FOR VISIT: Oncology follow-up, 2 months post-initiation of Lynparza for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) with BRCA2 germline mutation

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with mCRPC and germline BRCA2 mutation presenting for follow-up approximately 8 weeks after starting Lynparza (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily. He presents today for PSA check, labs review, and toxicity assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male, retired steelworker with a 45 pack-year smoking history (quit at age 62), who was originally diagnosed 4 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer - Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy and 8 discrete bone metastases at diagnosis. PSA at diagnosis was 187 ng/mL. Germline testing at diagnosis confirmed a BRCA2 p.E1308* pathogenic variant. His tumor also harbored TP53 p.R248Q, homozygous PTEN deletion, RB1 loss, and CDK12 frameshift on FoundationOne CDx.

He was initially treated with ADT (leup acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months) plus abi acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID, achieving an excellent PSA nadir of 1.1 ng/mL. He maintained castration-sensitive control for approximately 2 years before developing biochemical and radiographic progression consistent with castration-resistant disease at age 69 - PSA rising to 8.7 ng/mL with confirmed castrate testosterone, new bone lesions on bone scan, and nodal progression on CT.

He was transitioned to Xtandi 160 mg daily as second-line therapy, with initial PSA response to 1.4 ng/mL. He had manageable grade 1-2 fatigue and some mild word-finding difficulty on enza, and he was started on bone-directed therapy with zoledronic acid (dose-adjusted for CKD). After approximately 9 months on enza, repeat imaging demonstrated progression with new bone lesions (left iliac crest x2, right proximal humerus, sacrum) and enlarging pelvic and para-aortic nodes; PSA had risen to 14.3 ng/mL from nadir of 1.4 ng/mL.

Given his known BRCA2 germline mutation, he was transitioned to ola 300 mg PO BID (per PROfound trial data supporting PARP inhibitor use in BRCA-altered mCRPC). Prior to initiating ola, he received palliative radiotherapy to the right femoral neck (30 Gy in 10 fractions), with good pain response reducing right hip pain from 7/10 to approximately 2/10. Xtandi was discontinued at that time. ADT with Lupron was continued.

He started Lynparza approximately 8 weeks ago. He reports that his right hip pain has remained well-controlled since completing radiation, currently 1/10. He does note worsening fatigue compared to baseline, now rating it at a 6/10 in severity and describing it as heavier and more pervasive than what he experienced on Xtandi - he is sleeping approximately 10-11 hours per day and still feeling unrefreshed. He endorses new dyspnea on exertion, particularly when climbing stairs or walking more than one city block. He denies chest pain or palpitations at rest. He has had some nausea, primarily in the mornings, which he has been managing with ondansetron as needed - he estimates he is using it 3-4 times per week and finds it helpful. He denies vomiting. He endorses mild early satiety. Appetite is somewhat reduced but he is maintaining his oral intake.

His wife accompanies him today and confirms the fatigue is quite significant - she notes he spends much of the afternoon resting, but he is still independently mobile. He has had no new falls, no syncope, no neurological symptoms. No lower extremity edema new from prior. No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary obstruction symptoms.

He is currently on Lynparza 300 mg twice daily, Lupron acetate (last injection 6 weeks ago), zoledronic acid q12 weeks (last dose approximately 4 weeks ago), extended-release oxycodone 10 mg BID (initiated at prior visit for bone pain), acetaminophen 1g PRN, metformin 1000 mg daily (held briefly for Paraplatin - but note carbo not yet started, continuing metformin), lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily (started at last visit), and ondansetron 4 mg PRN nausea.

Review of his recent outside labs from 3 weeks ago shows hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, down from 9.6 g/dL at the time Lynparza was initiated. Today's full CBC is pending (drawn this morning).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Significant fatigue (grade 2-3 per patient description), mild weight loss of approximately 3 lbs since last visit. No fever or night sweats.
Cardiopulmonary: Dyspnea on exertion (new), no chest pain, no orthopnea, no PND. No cough.
Gastrointestinal: Nausea grade 1-2, managed with ondansetron. Decreased appetite. No vomiting. No diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. No new urinary obstructive symptoms. Continues to have decreased urinary stream at baseline.
Musculoskeletal: Right hip pain improved and well-controlled (1/10 post-radiation). Residual mild lumbar back pain at 2/10, stable. No new focal bone pain.
Neurological: No new word-finding difficulty (improved from enza). No headache, no focal weakness, no sensory changes. No visual changes.
Endocrine/metabolic: Hot flashes continue, manageable. Ongoing erectile dysfunction.
Hematologic: No bruising or bleeding. No epistaxis.
Psychiatric: Anxious about disease trajectory; coping with wife's support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM:

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 36.9 degrees C, oxygen saturation 95% on room air (previously 98% - note mild desaturation on exertion likely contributing to dyspnea symptoms), weight 176 lbs (down from 181 lbs at prior visit approximately 3 months ago).

General: Alert, oriented, cooperative. Mildly pale in appearance. In no acute distress. Moves slowly but independently.
HEENT: Conjunctival pallor noted bilaterally. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No S3 or S4. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles. Mild decrease in breath sounds at right base, possibly positional.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present.
Musculoskeletal: No pain on palpation of spine at L2 or T9. Right hip: no pain on passive range of motion (improved post-radiation). Left hip: mild discomfort on internal rotation, consistent with known left femoral head lesion.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Gait slow but stable.
Skin: No rash. No petechiae or ecchymoses.

---

LAB RESULTS (TODAY):

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 7.9 g/dL (decreased from 9.6 g/dL at ola initiation 8 weeks ago)
- Hematocrit: 24.1%
- MCV: 96 fL (normocytic)
- MCH: 31.2 pg
- Platelets: 168,000/uL (decreased from 194,000 at baseline)
- WBC: 4.1 K/uL
- ANC: 2.6 K/uL
- Reticulocyte count: 1.1% (hypoproliferative; absolute reticulocyte count low relative to degree of anemia)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.3 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL (eGFR ~42 mL/min/1.73m2, stable)
- BUN: 28 mg/dL
- ALT: 32 U/L
- AST: 38 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 194 U/L (down from ~287 at progression visit, but up from 112 at Xtandi response nadir - consistent with residual osseous disease)
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL
- Glucose: 118 mg/dL (fasting)

Other:
- LDH: 228 U/L (down from 412 at progression)
- PSA: 4.8 ng/mL (significant decline from 14.3 ng/mL at ola start, representing 66% reduction)
- Testosterone: 18 ng/dL (confirmed castrate levels)
- Iron studies: Ferritin 312 ng/mL, serum iron 58 mcg/dL, TIBC 280 mcg/dL, transferrin saturation 21% - pattern most consistent with anemia of chronic disease / functional iron deficiency in the context of bone marrow suppression rather than true iron-deficiency anemia

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging was obtained at this visit. The most recent restaging imaging (bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis at age 69.5 years on enza) showed partial response in osseous and nodal disease. Repeat imaging was not planned until approximately 4-5 months into Lynparza therapy or sooner if clinically indicated.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC), BRCA2 GERMLINE MUTATION - RESPONDING TO ola
Mr. Kowalski has a meaningful PSA response on Lynparza, with PSA down 66% from 14.3 to 4.8 ng/mL over 8 weeks. This is consistent with the substantial response rates seen in BRCA2-altered patients in PROfound - median PSA decline in BRCA1/2 cohorts suggests this response is real and clinically meaningful. Testosterone remains castrate-level. Clinically, his bone pain is markedly improved (helped by prior radiation to right femoral neck as well), and his overall disease-related symptoms have improved. We will continue Lynparza at current dose of 300 mg BID for now.

2. ola-ASSOCIATED ANEMIA - GRADE 2, WORSENING, REQUIRING MONITORING
Hemoglobin has declined from 9.6 g/dL at baseline to 7.9 g/dL today - a drop of 1.7 g/dL over 8 weeks. Reticulocyte count is 1.1%, indicating a hypoproliferative pattern consistent with Lynparza-induced bone marrow suppression rather than hemolysis or blood loss. Iron studies suggest functional iron deficiency / anemia of chronic disease rather than true iron deficiency - ferrous sulfate supplementation will be continued. MCV is 96 (normocytic), arguing against nutritional deficiency as primary etiology. He is symptomatic with exertional dyspnea and fatigue. His oxygen saturation at rest is 95%, which is mildly reduced and may be contributing to dyspnea. At this level (Hgb 7.9), he does not yet meet threshold criteria for transfusion (generally <7 g/dL or symptomatic with Hgb <8-8.5 depending on clinical judgment), but he is close to that threshold. We will check CBC in 2 weeks. If hemoglobin drops below 7.5 g/dL or he becomes more symptomatic, transfusion support will be initiated and dose reduction will be considered (first reduction to 200 mg BID per protocol). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not recommended in this setting given active malignancy. Bone marrow biopsy was discussed with patient to better characterize the marrow suppression; he declined at this time.

3. NAUSEA - GRADE 1-2, Lynparza-RELATED, CONTROLLED
ola-associated nausea is well documented and manageable. He is using ondansetron 3-4 times per week with adequate control. He denies vomiting. Oral intake is maintained. We will reinforce that taking Lynparza with food may reduce nausea. If not already doing so, we will have pharmacy counsel him on this.

4. FATIGUE - GRADE 2-3, MULTIFACTORIAL
Fatigue is significant and multifactorial: contributions from Lynparza, anemia, ADT/castrate state, deconditioning, and underlying disease burden. We will not reduce ola dose for fatigue alone at this point. Low-intensity physical activity (walking program) encouraged to the extent tolerated. Sleep hygiene counseling reinforced. Anemia management (see above) is the most actionable intervention for energy levels.

5. BONE METASTASES / PAIN MANAGEMENT
Right hip pain well controlled post-radiation (1/10). Mild lumbar pain 2/10, stable. Extended-release oxycodone 10 mg BID continued. Zoledronic acid ongoing per schedule (adjusted dose for CKD, eGFR ~42). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation confirmed (patient taking OTC supplement).

6. COMORBIDITIES
- Type 2 diabetes: Glucose 118 fasting, reasonably controlled. Metformin 1000 mg daily continued; no plans to escalate given CKD.
- Hypertension: BP 138/86 today, improved from prior. Lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily continued.
- CKD Stage 3b: Creatinine 1.7 mg/dL, eGFR ~42, stable. ola dose adjustment threshold is eGFR <31, so no change needed at this time. Continued monitoring. Zoledronic acid dose-adjusted. Metformin continued given eGFR >30 with monitoring.

---

PLAN:

1. Continue Lynparza (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID - no dose change at this time given PSA response and current anemia grade 2 (not yet meeting dose reduction threshold of grade 3).
2. Continue ADT: leup acetate 22.5 mg IM - next injection in approximately 6 weeks.
3. Continue zoledronic acid 4 mg IV q12 weeks for bone protection (dose-adjusted for CKD); ensure dental clearance is up to date.
4. Continue oxycodone ER 10 mg BID, oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN for breakthrough pain. Pain is well-managed; no escalation needed today.
5. Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily for functional iron supplementation.
6. Continue ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN nausea up to TID; counsel to take Lynparza with food.
7. Repeat CBC and CMP in 2 weeks. If hemoglobin <7.5 g/dL or worsening symptoms, patient will be contacted for transfusion evaluation and potential ola dose reduction to 200 mg BID.
8. Repeat PSA and full labs in 4 weeks at next scheduled visit.
9. Repeat restaging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan) planned at approximately 4-5 months on Lynparza therapy (approximately 2 months from now).
10. Continue current antihypertensive regimen; recheck BP at next visit.
11. Metformin continued; reinforce blood sugar monitoring given ola and prednisone (patient is no longer on prednisone - confirmed at this visit).
12. Reinforce exercise/activity as tolerated given fatigue. Occupational therapy referral offered for energy conservation strategies - patient interested, referral placed.
13. Social work to follow up given patient's reported anxiety; previously had social work involvement and found it beneficial.
14. Patient and wife updated on clinical status. They understand the PSA response is a positive sign and treatment is working. They understand the risk of progressive anemia and the need for close monitoring. All questions answered. Patient is in agreement with plan.

Return to clinic in 4 weeks or sooner if hemoglobin drops below threshold or symptoms worsen.

Electronically signed by Attending Genitourinary Medical Oncologist
